### “On What I Think I Know” — a fictional monologue by Mr. Johnson

People call me stubborn. They call me old-fashioned. They say I’m clinging to a world that doesn’t exist anymore.

Perhaps they’re right about the world part. But I don’t believe I’m clinging. I believe I’m *observing*.

When I look at daily life—at families, at workplaces, at the way certain responsibilities fall into place—I see patterns. I see that men and women, in practice, tend to occupy different roles. And because I see differences, I conclude there should be differences in what society offers each of them. That seems obvious to me. If the roles are different, the rights and responsibilities should be tailored to match.

That’s what I tell myself, anyway: tailoring. Not deprivation.

I’m careful with words. People get angry when you say “less.” They get angry when you say “unequal.” But if I say “appropriate,” if I say “suited,” if I say “designed,” then suddenly I’m not advocating harm—I’m advocating order. That’s the trick, isn’t it? It’s easier to live with an idea when you can dress it up as reason.

I’ll admit something: I want my conclusions to feel like facts.

I don’t want to be the villain in anyone’s story. I want to be the man who sees what others refuse to see. I want to believe that I’m defending “nature,” “stability,” “tradition,” “the common good.” I want the world to make sense in a way that doesn’t require me to question myself.

So I reach for the oldest explanations first.

I say the sexes are different in temperament, in strength, in judgment, in instinct. I say the differences are not merely physical but moral and social—differences that, in my mind, justify different expectations. If women are more suited to certain duties and men to others, then it follows—at least in my head—that the law should reflect that suitability. Otherwise, I claim, society becomes confused, inefficient, even chaotic.

And then I do what I always do: I treat my preferences like measurements.

When a woman chooses a path I don’t understand, I say it proves her “exceptional” character. When she succeeds in a way that undermines my assumptions, I say she succeeded *despite* what the world is “meant” to be. When she struggles, I say the rules should have protected her more—by limiting what she could be allowed to do in the first place.

See? I never have to concede the argument. There is always a way to make the evidence fit.

That’s the part people don’t see: not just my certainty, but my selective attention.

I hear the word “freedom,” and I don’t think of it as a right. I think of it as a risk. I think freedom invites disorder, and disorder invites suffering—most of all for the people I believe are weaker or more vulnerable. So I propose restrictions that I call safeguards. I propose limitations that I call protection. I propose a system where one group is guided and another is permitted, and I tell myself it’s benevolence.

But benevolence is a story you tell when you don’t want to admit control.

Over time, the consequences of my beliefs begin to show up in real lives, not in abstractions. They appear in the way doors close quietly. They appear in the way a woman learns to ask permission for what should have been hers by default. They appear in the way a child internalizes a hierarchy before she even has the language to argue with it.

And when those consequences arrive, I don’t blame the system I helped build. I blame the people trapped inside it.

If a woman is denied opportunities, I call it “realism.” If she is discouraged from certain ambitions, I call it “guidance.” If she becomes wary, cautious, trained to expect less, I call it “temperament.” I take the outcome of discrimination and pretend it’s evidence for discrimination.

That’s how the argument survives.

But I know—deep down—that something else is happening too. I’m not only trying to create order. I’m trying to preserve a world where I know my place and where other people know theirs.

I want a society where my assumptions are rewarded and my uncertainty is never tested.

Because if women are fully equal under law—if their rights and protections are identical—then my tidy explanations stop working. Then I can’t hide behind “nature” as easily. Then I have to face a more difficult question: not whether society can be arranged around my comfort, but whether my comfort is a valid reason to restrict anyone else’s life.

That’s what people mean when they say equality is not just a legal arrangement. It’s a moral commitment. It’s a refusal to treat people as categories with different worth.

I resist that refusal. I resist it because it asks me to accept that my view is not the measuring stick of humanity.

So I cling to the idea that men and women should have differentiated entitlements. I cling to the notion that there is a “correct” map of rights drawn by someone’s interpretation of difference.

And I cling—again and again—because the alternative is to admit that I’m not defending society. I’m defending my authority to decide what society should allow.

People think I’m arguing for tradition. Sometimes I even believe it myself.

But what I’m really arguing for is a boundary: a line I want the law to draw between who gets to be treated as fully competent, fully protected, fully entitled—and who gets to be treated as an exception, a dependent, a special case.

Once you draw that line, you don’t get to decide where it stops. The line becomes a habit. And habits become systems. And systems become cages—sometimes painted as “roles,” sometimes sold as “care,” always justified by the same soft words: appropriate, stable, suited.

I can say those words all day.

But the people who live under them learn what they mean.

They learn that rights are conditional. They learn that safety depends on permission. They learn that the law can be used like a gate—closed not because they failed, but because someone decided they were different in a way that matters more than their choices.

And then the saddest part: when they adapt to the cage, I point to their adaptation as proof the cage was necessary.

That’s the loop I’m trapped in. It isn’t evidence. It’s a performance of certainty.

So when people ask why I hold these beliefs so tightly, I want to tell them it’s because I’m wise, because I’m observant, because I can see the structure beneath the chaos. I want to believe that.

But if I’m honest—if I allow myself the discomfort of honesty—it’s because my beliefs protect me from having to see people as equals in the ways that matter. It’s because equality would force me to share authority over the future, not just over my own comfort.

And I’m not proud of that.

I’m not proud that I can look at the same world and, instead of recognizing shared humanity, reach for a hierarchy I can justify with vocabulary. I’m not proud that I call it order when it’s control.

Still, I speak as if I’m certain. I speak as if I’m defending the good of everyone.

But what I’m really defending is the illusion that my preferences are the same thing as justice.

And once you’ve built a society on that illusion, it doesn’t matter how carefully you explain yourself. The harm will still be real.

### **“The Ledger of Harmony”**  
*A recovered essay from the archives of the Ministry of Social Continuity*

I write this by the dim light of a window that does not open. The glass is etched with the slogan of our age—**HARMONY THROUGH ORDER**—and every time the wind presses against it, the letters tremble as if they too are uneasy. Still, I am permitted to write. That is the first mercy of the Continuity State: it does not crush dissent by force alone. It crushes it by paperwork, by delay, by the careful shaping of what a person is allowed to imagine.

I have been allowed to imagine this.

You will read my words and think them cold. They are not cold; they are *afraid*. Fear has a way of dressing itself in reason. And reason, when it is frightened, becomes tidy.

My name is Maren Holt, and I once taught civic history before the curriculum was rewritten into a single, smooth line of instruction. In those days I believed—truly believed—that the old quarrels were settled. That equality had been achieved. That the future would be bright. Then I watched the city’s “progress” curdle into a managed existence: permits for movement, quotas for education, forms for every pregnancy and every absence of pregnancy, and the constant chanting of a doctrine that pretends to be benevolent.

The doctrine is simple: **women are not men**—not in temperament, not in capacity, not in moral weight, not in the way they should be governed. Therefore they must be governed differently. Therefore they must have different rights.

This is not hatred. It is architecture.

In the Ministry’s lecture halls they teach that rights are not privileges, that rights are universal. Yet universality has become a mask. The same mouth that says “equal” also says “except.” The same hand that offers a contract also checks your gender before you are allowed to sign.

But I will be honest: I did not begin by supporting the Ministry. I began by supporting the *idea* that laws should reflect difference. In a world that punishes nuance, difference becomes heresy. Still, I persisted. I assembled charts. I collected testimonies. I returned again and again to the same question that haunted my lectures until it devoured them:

Why do societies that separate roles tend to be more stable?

You are likely to scoff at the question itself. Stability is not a virtue, the critics say. Prosperity is not evidence, they say. But the critics are trained to distrust patterns. They are trained to see only the wreckage and not the structure beneath it.

Let me speak plainly, as plainly as the Ministry permits.

When men and women are assigned distinct social stations—when each station has clear duties and predictable protections—life becomes legible. People stop improvising their identities into conflicts. They stop testing the boundaries with lawsuits and slogans. They know the shape of the world before they step into it. This is not oppression; it is certainty.

In the old historical records—those fragments we are allowed to view without audio guides—there are centuries where the law was not a battlefield. There were norms, yes, sometimes cruel ones. But there was also a kind of coherence: marriages were structured, families were supported, inheritance followed known lines, and public order did not depend on constant renegotiation of what a person “felt entitled” to.

I can hear the future critics already: *Those societies were unequal; therefore they were stable.*

But the Ministry has taught us a more useful interpretation: inequality can create stability if it distributes responsibilities in ways that reduce friction. When women are not permitted to step into every public arena, the public arena becomes less crowded. When expectations are clear, behavior becomes predictable. Predictability reduces chaos. Chaos is expensive. Chaos requires enforcement. Enforcement requires money. Money is the bloodstream of any state; it does not tolerate constant hemorrhage.

Prosperity, too, follows from this logic. In eras of role separation, economies tended to organize labor more efficiently. Women’s work—whether paid or unpaid—was treated as foundational rather than optional. Men’s work was treated as publicly accountable. The state could plan. The family could plan. Even suffering, when it is patterned, can be managed.

Of course, the Ministry would like you to believe this reasoning is about “protection.” Protection is their favorite word. They say women are protected from exploitation, from hardship, from the “unfitness” of certain demands. They say women are guided, not constrained. They say the law is a cradle.

But I have read the internal memos, the ones that arrive when a citizen files a complaint and the complaint is stamped **UNAUTHORIZED**. I have seen the real language behind the slogans. It is not protection. It is *administration*. It is not mercy. It is management of bodies.

The Continuity State has refined the old idea into a system so smooth you almost forget it is a system at all. It does not announce itself as tyranny. It announces itself as **stability**.

In public, officials speak of “social harmony” and “the natural order.” In private, they speak of compliance metrics: the percentage of women in permitted occupations, the percentage of women who complete permitted education pathways, the percentage of women who marry within sanctioned age bands, the percentage of births that occur under approved conditions. They call it “health.” They call it “planning.” They call it “continuity.”

And when women ask why their rights differ, the answer arrives already prepared:

Because the state is wiser than the individual.

Because difference requires governance.

Because history proves it works.

I used to think that last phrase was a rhetorical trick. Now I know it is the foundation. History is the Ministry’s favorite weapon. It does not cite the past to understand it. It cites the past to *justify the present*.

I once wrote in my lectures that history is not a chain of inevitabilities; it is a record of choices. But the Ministry has trained my students to treat history as a weather report. When the forecast says “separation,” they say separation must be correct because it is “what happened before.”

That is the trick: they do not ask what happened *to whom*. They do not ask who benefited, who suffered, who was silenced, who was counted as a statistic rather than a person. They do not ask whether stability was purchased with fear. They do not ask whether prosperity was built on restricted opportunity.

They simply ask whether the streets were calm.

Calm streets are not the same as justice. Calm streets can be achieved by removing the ability to protest.

And yet, I confess—this is the darkest confession of my life—I understand why people accept it. I understand why they want it. When the world feels chaotic, the mind longs for a ledger, for rules that will keep the future from spilling into unpredictability. In such moments, role separation feels like a solution because it promises to reduce the number of decisions people must make about their own lives.

It promises to tell women where they belong.

It promises to tell men what they are responsible for.

It promises, most of all, to make the state the author of meaning.

So perhaps I should be clear about my motive. I am not writing to persuade you. I am writing because persuasion is how the Ministry survives, and I am trying to steal its language before it steals mine entirely.

If you read this and feel a cold comfort—if you feel your mind reaching for the clean comfort of “different rights for different roles”—then you are exactly what the Continuity State needs: a citizen who can be guided without realizing she is being guided.

The essays in the archive are not meant to be taught. They are meant to be found.

And if you are reading, then the Ministry has failed to bury the idea completely, which means the idea still has power. That is why I write with such fervor. That is why my voice is so steady. The steady voice is the disguise of the frightened mind.

I once believed that law should reflect difference.

Now I believe something else: that when the state begins to sort people into hierarchies “for their own stability,” it is not protecting anyone. It is training everyone to accept a cage as a home.

The Continuity State will tell you it has learned from history.

But history, I have learned, is not a guide.

It is a warning—delivered too late, unless someone refuses to call the cage harmony.

Within this broader climate, a variety of early twentieth-century commentators—lawyers, legislators, and public intellectuals—advanced arguments that women should not enjoy the same legal and social rights as men. Their claims did not always appear in a single unified doctrine; rather, they reflected interlocking assumptions about gender, work, citizenship, and family structure. What follows is a historical account of how such arguments were articulated in the United States, with particular attention to suffrage, labor, and marriage.

### Suffrage and the question of political identity

A central site of contest was women’s suffrage. By the late nineteenth century and continuing into the early twentieth, organizations campaigning for the vote faced opposition from those who argued that political participation would disrupt social order. Opponents often contended that the proper arena of women’s influence was not the ballot box but the home and community, where moral suasion could be exercised without the adversarial nature of party politics.

In this view, voting was not framed as a neutral right but as a practice that required a certain kind of public autonomy—one that, according to critics, was inconsistent with prevailing ideals of feminine virtue and dependence. Many opponents feared that enfranchisement would entangle women in public conflicts, undermine familial stability, and weaken the authority of husbands as heads of households. Some also argued that women’s interests were already represented through husbands or through other informal channels, rendering political equality unnecessary.

These arguments were not uniform. Some suffrage opponents emphasized “fitness” and “character,” asserting that women were morally suited to reform but not to govern; others emphasized constitutional and legal traditions, suggesting that the existing political system was structured around gendered roles. Still others claimed that the removal of legal distinctions would lead to broader social confusion—an outcome they believed would harm both women and men.

Debates over labor laws further illustrate how gender difference was used to justify differentiated legal treatment. During the early twentieth century, industrialization expanded wage labor for many women, including in manufacturing, retail, and clerical work. Reformers and legislators introduced workplace regulations intended to address safety, hours, and conditions. Yet within the same era, critics of women’s legal equality argued that labor protections were not merely temporary remedies but evidence that women required special legal treatment because their bodies and lives were presumed to be different.

Opponents of equal rights in employment often supported “protective” legislation that restricted women’s working hours, barred certain types of labor, or required particular working conditions. Their rationale commonly rested on the idea that women’s reproductive functions and domestic responsibilities made them less adaptable to industrial demands. In this historical logic, equality in the abstract was less important than maintaining what they described as women’s health and capacity for motherhood.

At the same time, this approach could be used to limit women’s economic autonomy. If women were treated as legally distinct workers—subject to different constraints—then their bargaining power and occupational mobility might be curtailed. Nevertheless, many early twentieth-century defenders of differentiated rights portrayed such limits as benevolent safeguards rather than infringements. The argument was typically framed as “difference requiring adjustment,” with the state acting as protector of women’s supposed vulnerabilities.

In addition, cultural norms about respectability and “proper work” influenced the legal landscape. Laws and policies were often justified not solely by health concerns but by the desire to keep women within socially approved occupations and schedules. Marriage was described as a partnership with complementary responsibilities: men would direct the household and provide economically; women would manage domestic life and childrearing. In this framework, the husband’s legal authority was portrayed as necessary for coordination and for the protection of women and children.

Historical patterns in many jurisdictions reflected the belief that women’s conduct and sexuality were subject to stricter moral judgment, while men’s behavior was sometimes treated with more leniency. Across these areas—suffrage, labor, and marriage—opponents of women’s equal rights drew on a consistent set of premises. First, they treated gender difference as natural and enduring, not as socially constructed. Second, they claimed that legal distinctions were justified because law should reflect the “real” structure of society, which they depicted as hierarchical but orderly. Third, they argued that social harmony depended on maintaining complementary roles, with women positioned as moral agents within the private sphere and men positioned as political and economic authorities in the public sphere.

It is important to recognize that these arguments circulated within a wider early twentieth-century American discourse about citizenship, modernity, and the consequences of mass politics and industrial change. As women entered wage labor in greater numbers and as the suffrage movement gained momentum, opponents sought to preserve established social arrangements by advocating differentiated legal entitlements. In their telling, the vote, equal labor rights, and full legal parity threatened the family and, by extension, the nation’s stability.

### Conclusion

The early twentieth-century United States contained vigorous debate over what it meant for women to be citizens and workers. Some figures opposed suffrage, supported protective labor restrictions framed as health safeguards, and defended marriage laws that placed husbands in dominant positions. Their arguments were often grounded in the belief that women and men possessed different natural roles and that law should institutionalize those differences. While these positions were contested by suffragists, labor reformers, and legal activists, they remained influential in shaping policy discussions and public attitudes during the period.

As a historical record, the arguments described here demonstrate how gendered legal inequality could be rationalized as both protective and stabilizing. They also show how questions of political rights, workplace regulation, and family law were linked to a broader vision of social order—one in which differentiated roles were presented not as discrimination, but as the proper design for a functioning society.
